mappings.rationale: Directly covers African history including colonial rule, nationalist movements, independence, and post-independence developments discussed throughout subsections D and E.
Directly relevant to the political developments in sub-Saharan Africa including one-party states, military coups, and democratic transitions discussed in subsections E.4-8.
Relevant to the separatist church movements and religious dimensions of African societies under colonial rule and after independence discussed in subsection E.8.a.iii.
Directly relevant to the antiapartheid movement, the African National Congress, and the establishment of black majority rule in South Africa discussed in subsection E.8.b.i.
Directly covers the partition of Palestine, establishment of Israel, Arab-Israeli wars, the intifada, and peace processes discussed in subsection B.3.c.
Directly covers Iran since 1925 including the Pahlavi regimes, the 1978-79 Revolution, the Islamic Republic, and the Iran-Iraq war discussed in subsection C.1.
Relevant to the Islamic dimensions of politics across Southwest Asia and North Africa including Islamic fundamentalism in Egypt, Iran's Islamic Republic, and Islamic law in Sudan.
Relevant to the role of Islam across the region from the Arab states to North Africa and the rise of Islamic fundamentalism discussed throughout subsections B, C, and D.
Relevant to the introduction of Islamic law in Sudan, the Islamic Republic of Iran, and the role of Islamic governance across the region.
Provides historical context for understanding the Islamic civilizations and political structures of Southwest Asia and North Africa.
Directly relevant to the rise of Islamic fundamentalism in Egypt, Tunisia, Algeria, Iran, and the broader region discussed in subsections B.3.e, C.1.c, and D.2.
Relevant to terrorism in the region including PLO activities, the intifada, and the disruptive role of Libya discussed in subsections B.3, C.1, and D.2.a.
Relevant to the establishment of Israel, Jewish settlement in Palestine, and the Zionist movement discussed in subsection B.1.c and B.3.c.
Directly relevant to the Balfour Declaration, Jewish settlement in Palestine, the Biltmore Resolution, and the establishment of Israel discussed in subsection B.1.c.
Relevant to the historical context of Jewish persecution that drove Zionist settlement in Palestine and the establishment of Israel discussed in subsection B.1.c.
Central to the entire section which traces the end of European colonial rule and emergence of new nations across Southwest Asia and Africa.
Relevant to British mandate territories (Palestine, Iraq, Transjordan), British colonies in Africa, and the dissolution of British colonial rule discussed throughout.
Relevant to the European colonial empires in Africa and Southwest Asia whose dissolution is the central narrative of this section.
Relevant to the postcolonial challenges faced by newly independent African and Southwest Asian nations including economic development problems and ethnic conflicts.
Relevant to the rise of African nationalist parties, Arab nationalism, Kurdish separatism, and the independence movements throughout the section.
Relevant to numerous civil wars including the Lebanese civil war, Angolan and Mozambican civil wars, Somali clan warfare, and Sudanese civil war discussed throughout.
Relevant to the numerous conflicts including the Arab-Israeli wars, the Iran-Iraq War, the Persian Gulf wars, and the Algerian War of Independence.
Relevant to apartheid, political persecution, ethnic strife, genocide in Rwanda and Burundi, and human rights issues across the region.
Relevant to the massive refugee flows from conflicts across the region including Palestinian refugees, Somali refugees, and displacement from civil wars in Africa.
Relevant to the migration patterns including Jewish immigration to Israel, labor migration in the Arabian Peninsula, and displacement from African conflicts.
Relevant to communist and socialist movements in Africa including Ethiopian socialism under Mengistu and Soviet involvement in African conflicts discussed in subsection E.
Relevant to the struggles for democratic governance across Africa from military coups and one-party states to multiparty elections discussed throughout subsection E.
Relevant to the numerous authoritarian regimes discussed including Ataturk, Nasser, Qaddafi, Mobutu, apartheid South Africa, and military governments across Africa.
Directly relevant to apartheid in South Africa, racial discrimination in Rhodesia, and the racial dimensions of colonialism discussed throughout subsection E.8.
Relevant to the diplomatic dimensions including the mandate system, the Camp David Accords, peace talks, and international negotiations discussed throughout.
Relevant to the international dimensions of regional conflicts including superpower involvement, UN interventions, and multilateral peacekeeping.
Relevant to the mandate system, UN trusteeship, international sanctions, and the legal frameworks governing decolonization and self-determination.
Relevant to UN involvement including peacekeeping in the Congo, intervention in Somalia, sanctions against Libya and South Africa, and the mandate system.
Relevant to superpower involvement in African conflicts, Soviet and Cuban military presence, and Cold War dynamics in Southwest Asia.
Relevant to the geopolitical significance of oil in the Arabian Peninsula, the strategic importance of the Suez Canal, and Cold War competition in Africa.
Relevant to the Egyptian revolution (1952), the Iranian revolution (1978-79), the Algerian War of Independence, and other revolutionary transformations discussed throughout.
Relevant to the economic development challenges, oil-based economies, and the transition from colonial to independent economic systems discussed throughout.
Relevant to the economic transformation of the region including oil discovery in the Arabian Peninsula, colonial economics, and postcolonial development challenges.
Relevant to the widespread poverty, economic difficulties, and development challenges facing newly independent African nations discussed in subsections E.4-8.
Relevant to the economic development challenges, aid programs, and problems of newly independent states across Africa and Southwest Asia.
Relevant to WWII's impact on the region including North African campaigns, Allied occupation of Iran, and the war's role in accelerating decolonization.
Relevant to Fascist Italian domination of Libya (1922-42) and the Italian conquest of Ethiopia (1936) discussed in subsections D.1.b and E.5.a.
Relevant to the post-WWI mandate system, the division of former German colonies, and the dissolution of the Ottoman Empire discussed in subsections B.1, D.1, and E.1.
Relevant to the nuclear dimension of regional security, Israel's nuclear capability, and France's nuclear testing discussed in the context of regional conflicts.
Relevant to the socialist and Ba'thist ideologies in Syria and Iraq, Ethiopian socialism, and socialist movements across Africa discussed throughout.
Relevant to the ideological dimensions of regional politics including pan-Arabism, Ba'thism, Kemalism, and African socialism discussed throughout.
Relevant to the AIDS pandemic in southern Africa mentioned in subsection E.8.b.iii in the context of Botswana, Lesotho, and Swaziland.
Relevant to the public health crises including the AIDS pandemic affecting southern Africa discussed in subsection E.8.b.iii.
Relevant to the organized crime and corruption associated with oil resources and political instability across the region.
Relevant to populist leaders including Nasser in Egypt and various African leaders who mobilized mass support for independence and post-independence governance.
Relevant to the interactions among Judaism, Christianity, and Islam that shape conflicts in the region, particularly the Arab-Israeli conflict and sectarian tensions in Lebanon.
Relevant to the role of the Orthodox Church in Ethiopia and the Christian dimension of the Lebanese civil war discussed in subsections B.3.a and E.5.a.
Relevant to the Christian-Muslim dynamics in Nigeria, Sudan, and Lebanon, and the missionary presence in colonial Africa discussed throughout.
Relevant to the settlement of borders in the mandate system, the partition of Palestine, and the numerous territorial disputes across Africa and Southwest Asia.
Relevant to the multireligious political system in Lebanon, ethnic diversity in African states, and the challenges of multi-ethnic nation-building discussed throughout.
Relevant to the Jewish diaspora and return to Israel, Palestinian diaspora, and the African diaspora context discussed in subsections B.1.c and B.3.c.
Relevant to the social changes and status of women in the context of secularization under Ataturk and the suppression of Western influence under Khomeini.
Relevant to the gendered dimensions of colonial and postcolonial societies in Africa and Southwest Asia.
Relevant to the numerous military conflicts including the Arab-Israeli wars, the Iran-Iraq War, and counterinsurgency campaigns discussed throughout.
Relevant to the post-Cold War emergence of South Africa and other African nations as important actors in the international system.
Relevant to federal structures in Nigeria, Ethiopia's regional federalism, and the federation experiments in Rhodesia and East Africa discussed throughout.
Relevant to Ataturk's secularization program in Turkey and the tension between secular and religious governance across the region.
Covers the Gulf War, Iraq War, and the rise of ISIS, directly relevant to this section's treatment of Southwest Asian history and the emergence of new political orders.
